text:
It is, of course, the universe's fault, and here is the full story.

When the toast was bread, it had no enemies. It sat in the bag, innocent and unaware, dreaming of being a sandwich. Then someone buttered it, and the moment that butter touched its surface, the toast entered into an ancient cosmic contract it never signed but is nevertheless legally bound by.

The contract reads, in very small print distributed across seventeen dimensions: "Any foodstuff improved by human effort shall, upon falling, orient itself so as to maximize human suffering." Butter is an improvement. Therefore the suffering is mandatory.

Now here is the physics of it, which scientists will tell you involves table height, rotational momentum, and the roughly one-meter drop from a typical counter being just enough for the toast to complete half a rotation. This is technically correct but completely misses the point, because the table height is not a coincidence. The table height is that height precisely because the universe measured it out in advance. Early humans, under subtle cosmic influence they mistook for ergonomic preference, built their tables at exactly the height required to guarantee maximum dairy-side impact. The toast does not land butter-side down because of physics. Physics was designed around the toast.

The butter itself is complicit. It has been waiting its whole life for this moment. It did not ask to be churned. It did not ask to be spread. But now that it is on the toast, airborne, briefly free, it commits fully to the bit. It pulls the toast toward the floor with a gravitational enthusiasm that Newton, had he been paying attention, would have listed as a fifth fundamental force: the attraction between buttered surfaces and dirty kitchen floors.

And the floor, of course, has been practicing.
